title: Higher-Order Token Interactions via Quantum Attention
authors: Jian Xu, Chao Li, Delu Zeng, John Paisley, Qibin Zhao
abstract: Standard dot-product self-attention computes, in a single layer, only pairwise (order-2) interactions between tokens; representing a generic order-$k$ interaction is known to require either super-quadratic resources in one layer or composition across depth. We introduce \textbf{Quantum Higher-Order Attention (QHA)}, a shallow, hardware-realizable quantum attention head that, via data re-uploading and an all-to-all non-Clifford entangler, synthesizes order-$k$ token interactions inside the circuit and exposes them through a local single-qubit read-out. We prove (i) an expressivity separation: any single standard self-attention layer with embedding dimension $m$, $H$ heads and $p$-bit precision satisfying $mHp=o(N/\log\log N)$ cannot represent the order-$k$ correlation family that one QHA head represents with circuit depth $O(\log k)$ ($O(k)$ two-qubit gates); and (ii) a trainability guarantee for its local-design instantiation: with a local read-out and $O(\log n)$ depth the gradient variance is $Ω(1/\mathrm{poly}(n))$ (no barren plateau), which we confirm empirically -- while being explicit that the more expressive all-to-all instantiation we benchmark is trained empirically and shows exponentially decaying gradients. Empirically, at a $6.5\times$ smaller parameter budget, QHA generalizes hidden-subset parity of every order $k\le6$ from disjoint inputs, whereas the larger classical attention head collapses past order~2; consistent with theory, the size of the advantage tracks the target's Fourier degree - largest for parity and shrinking when low-order structure is present. As an application, QHA serves as a compact high-order interaction detector across three domains - genetic epistasis, learning-parity-with-noise, and graph triangle detection - reaching the noise ceiling at the smallest parameter budget where field-standard linear methods fail.
summary: This paper introduces Quantum Higher-Order Attention (QHA), a shallow quantum circuit designed to model interactions of arbitrary order $k$. It theoretically proves that QHA can represent correlations beyond the capability of standard single-layer classical attention mechanisms. Empirically, QHA demonstrates superior performance and parameter efficiency when detecting complex, high-order feature dependencies in tasks like genetic epistasis.
main_problem: Standard self-attention mechanisms are fundamentally limited to pairwise (order-2) interactions, making it difficult to model higher-order correlations ($k>2$) efficiently.
main_result: The proposed Quantum Higher-Order Attention (QHA) head can synthesize and represent order-$k$ token interactions using a shallow quantum circuit depth $O(\log k)$, achieving an expressivity separation from classical attention layers.
method: The QHA synthesizes high-order correlations through data re-uploading, an all-to-all non-Clifford entangler, and a local single-qubit read-out measurement.
model_or_system: The model is the Quantum Higher-Order Attention (QHA) head, which operates on $n$ qubits representing tokens. It utilizes specific quantum gates (RZ, RZZ) to encode high-order entanglement before measuring a local single-qubit expectation value.
key_observables: Test accuracy on high-order tasks (e.g., parity), parameter efficiency ($	ext{acc} - 0.5) / 	ext{params}$, and the order of interaction ($k$).
important_parameters: Circuit depth $O(\log k)$, number of qubits $n$, and the target interaction order $k$.
main_assumptions: The advantage demonstrated is an inductive-bias and representational separation, not necessarily an exponential speedup; also, the local read-out forces the high-order structure to be synthesized internally.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the QHA circuit components (encoder, entangler, readout) and compare performance metrics like accuracy across varying interaction orders ($k$) for classical vs. quantum models.
paper_structure: The paper establishes a theoretical expressivity separation using communication complexity arguments, proves trainability via barren plateau analysis, and then validates the concept empirically by applying QHA to high-order tasks like epistasis detection on both simulators and real hardware.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete quantum circuit architecture (QHA) that explicitly targets overcoming known limitations in classical machine learning models regarding feature interaction complexity, which is relevant for modeling complex physical systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: QHA head: per-qubit data re-uploading encoder, an all-to-all non-Clifford RZZ entangler, and a local single-qubit read-out feeding a linear head; L blocks. The order-k interaction is synthe- sised inside the circuit and exposed on a single-qubit marginal.
Figure 2: An explicit QHA circuit (n=4, L=1): Hadamards and RZ data re-uploading, all �4 2  =6 RZZ couplings (all-to-all), a trainable RY layer, and a single-qubit ⟨Z⟩read-out (not shown).

Figure 3: Test accuracy vs. interaction order k (disjoint split, n=12, mean±std over 7–8 seeds for parity (left, matching Table 1) and 3 for the junta (right)). Left (hidden-subset parity): all Fourier mass sits at degree k, so the classical head (1922 params) collapses toward chance for k ≥3 while QHA (296 params) stays at ceiling — a maximal separation. Right (generic order-k junta): the random target carries low-degree mass that the low-degree-biased classical head can exploit, so it stays near ceiling and only begins to drop at k=5; QHA again holds at ceiling. The gap thus tracks the target’s Fourier degree, exactly as the theory predicts.

Figure 4: Order-k vs. qubit-count n phase diagram (parity, mean test accuracy). Classical attention (left) is reliable only at order 2; QHA (middle) is at ceiling almost everywhere (n=8 is data-limited by the 256-point cube); the difference (right) localizes the quantum advantage to the high-order region.

Figure 5: Hardware qubit-scaling on IBM Heron: hardware accuracy (blue) tracks the noiseless simulator (grey) up to n=10 and stays ≥0.95 through n=14, even as the raw ⟨Z⟩correlation (red) decays — the linear read-out absorbs the noise.
